speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Your fasting blood glucose today is 5.9 mmol/L — that's within the target range for someone with type 2 diabetes.

speaker: patient
text: Good morning, Doctor. Yes, I’ve been trying to keep my diet consistent and walking daily.

speaker: doctor
text: Great to hear. You’re currently on Metformin 500 mg twice daily — we’ll continue that for now while monitoring your response.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve had no stomach upset so far, which I was worried about.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s reassuring. Your systolic blood pressure today reads 136 mmHg — slightly elevated but not urgent.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 152 g/L — well within normal limits for your age and sex.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve noticed some swelling in my ankles lately, especially by evening. It goes down overnight.

speaker: patient
text: It’s been happening over the past week or so — lower limb edema, mostly in both feet and calves.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your history and current findings, your primary diagnosis remains Coronary Artery Disease — we’ll need to assess whether this edema relates to cardiac function or other factors like medication or renal status.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also felt a bit more fatigued than usual, though I’m not sure if that’s related.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s review your labs and vitals again closely. We may need to adjust your diabetes management to better support cardiovascular health.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m going to increase your Metformin to 1000 mg twice daily — this should improve glycemic control without increasing gastrointestinal risk, given your tolerance so far.

speaker: doctor
text: Interestingly, your repeat hemoglobin came back at 169 g/L — higher than the 152 we saw earlier today. That suggests possible lab variability or hemoconcentration; we’ll recheck it next visit.

speaker: patient
text: Oh — I did drink less water yesterday, so maybe that’s why?

speaker: doctor
text: That could explain it. Also, your systolic pressure just measured 114 mmHg — quite a drop from the 136 we recorded earlier. Were you seated quietly for five minutes before this reading?

speaker: patient
text: Yes — and actually, that reading matches the 5.9 mmol/L from your first test this morning. My home meter gave me the same number just before I left.

speaker: doctor
text: Consistent glucose readings are encouraging. Let’s also consider checking BNP and a basic metabolic panel to rule out early heart failure contributing to the edema.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. I’ve also been checking my BP at home — sometimes it’s higher, sometimes lower.

speaker: doctor
text: Home monitoring is helpful. Just make sure you’re using proper technique — cuff size, arm position, rest time. We’ll go over that before you leave.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked again right before coming in, my systolic was 136 mmHg — same as your first reading. So maybe the 114 was an outlier.
